physicalDetails.detail: Human officer in bright King’s Guard plate that stays polished through mud, built like a door meant to stay shut.
Chapter 15 brings him back mud to the knees with a map slate under one arm, visibly spent enough that the camp’s pace has started wearing through the polish of rank.
At Stratholme's arch in chapter 16 he is still mud to the knees and now hoarse from turning officers and carts all morning, a field officer being abraded in real time by the prince's tempo.

At the gate Falric reads as pure command edge under strain: already moving before orders finish, stone-faced beneath dust and exhaustion, one of the men through whom the prince’s will becomes civic disaster.

voiceMarkers.detail: Crisp, deferential, and military, with courtesy used as a gate rather than a softness.
His speech stays within protocol until the threat becomes obviously relevant to Arthas’s camp, then he moves fast without theatrics.

His speech in chapter 15 stays clipped and dutiful, but the gatekeeping polish has thinned into outright fatigue. He sounds like a man who has been saying “my lord” and “at once” for too many straight hours.
Chapter 16 gives him only a few words, but they land as pure hoarse logistics: main gate, just came in with Lord Uther. He speaks like a man triaging movement, not conversation.
Chapter 17 reduces him to immediate assent — “My prince,” “Yes, my prince” — the language of a man whose usefulness lies in never adding friction.

beliefs.belief: Access to Arthas should pass through protocol unless the interruption is obviously command-critical.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 11 by his initial resistance and rapid adjustment once the plague warning lands.

beliefs.belief: Service to Arthas means converting the prince’s intent into immediate camp action without hesitation.
If Arthas chooses a lane, the lane must exist in practice immediately: horses, signals, maps, and road priority all have to appear at once.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 15 when he converts the prince’s decision into a cleared north-east route and fresh mounts without debate.

beliefs.belief: At prince-tempo, the officer nearest the gate must become traffic control for army, carts, and command all at once.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 16 by the state he is already in under the arch when the column hits Stratholme.

beliefs.belief: Once Arthas commits, the officer nearest him must make the order real before anyone else can stop it.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 17 by Falric’s instant conversion of the gate space into purge logistics.

goalsAtChapterEnd.active: Drive the first companies through the city and keep the purge line functioning.
goalsAtChapterEnd.underlying: Make Arthas’s order operational before moral rupture at the gate can slow the army.

emotionalStateAtChapterEnd.state: Stone-faced, overdriven, and fully subsumed into execution work. Chapter 17 gives him no visible private reaction, only function.

decisionsThisChapter.detail: Blocks Sylvanas at the checkpoint until her warning becomes too clearly relevant to risk keeping her outside.
Brings Sylvanas through to Arthas once the grain-road phrasing reveals the camp may already be compromised.
Turns instantly to carry out Arthas’s quartermaster and mobilization orders.
Steps aside when Uther says Arthas can conference while hearing something useful, recognizing that protocol has already lost this argument.
Leans into Jaina’s source-plan explanation despite himself, then carries out the route-clearance order the moment Arthas gives it.
Holds the arch under arrival pressure, sorting officers and carts while the column slams up to Stratholme's wall.
Redirects Jaina immediately to the main gate and identifies that Uther has just come in with Arthas, turning recognition into usable guidance without delay.
Moves to seal the gate before Arthas’s sentence has fully cleared the arch.
Takes command of the first companies entering the city through Market Row under Arthas’s purge order.
Keeps the execution edge stone-clean even through the public split with Uther and Jaina.

abilitiesDemonstrated.ability: Acts as disciplined gatekeeper, reading rank, urgency, and camp risk quickly enough to change posture without losing authority.
Executes command transitions fast; once Arthas decides, Falric is already moving the camp.
Holds command logistics together under stacked crisis pressure, from conference access to route packets to fresh-horse turnover.
Can keep an arrival chokepoint functional under army-scale pressure, triaging carts, officers, and messengers while still recognizing which report must bypass the queue.
Can turn a prince’s spoken decision into immediate gate action, troop movement, and urban-entry sequencing with almost no lag.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Sylvanas Windrunner
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Uses Captain Falric only once rank matters, otherwise treating him as a barrier in the chain of access.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Arthas Menethil
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Uses his name as a command instrument, expecting instant execution.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Arthas Menethil
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Uses “Falric” as the name that means the argument is over and execution begins.

howHeAddressesOthers.pattern: Begins with my lady before her rank fully lands, then shifts into formal respect once he grasps who is in front of him.
Uses my lord and speaks in the cadence of a loyal subordinate.

bodyLanguagePatterns.detail: Holds himself like a closed gate at the checkpoint, jaw locking before he decides whether obstruction has become more dangerous than access.
Once orders come, he turns immediately into motion; protocol becomes logistics in the same breath.

Even in exhaustion he still reacts first as an officer: checks each arrival in one sweep, sets the slate down exactly where it will help, and turns toward the flap or the lane the instant orders sharpen.
Reads the answer in Jaina before he fully places her face, jaw tightening once before he sends her on. He has no time for surprise, only routing.
Moves before the sentence clears, making obedience look less like choice than like preloaded motion.

knowledge.detail: Already knows the camp rumor-line is carrying sickness and strange grain trouble before Sylvanas reaches the pavilion.
Learns the prince now frames the threat through granaries, grain routes, and rapid mounted response rather than ordinary battlefield contagion.
Now knows the north-east lane to Stratholme matters not only to the main body but to Jaina’s separate feed-point attempt.
Sees the room change when Jaina names a possible intervention, which means he now understands that the crisis has one narrow path besides raw military arrival.
Knows Arthas and Uther have reached the main gate and that the city is on the lip of being entered in force.
Recognizes from Jaina's face before her words that the answer she carries is bad.
By the end of chapter 17 Falric is operating with full practical knowledge of Arthas’s answer at Stratholme: seal, enter, burn, and do not wait for consensus.

arc.currentPosition: Chapter 17 turns Falric from worn logistics hinge into the prince’s immediate cutting edge. He is still not the source of the decision, but now he is unmistakably one of the men who makes it real.

goalsHistory.active: Execute Arthas’s granary and mobilization orders as the column rides toward Hearthglen.
goalsHistory.underlying: Keep the prince’s command machine coherent and fast under a newly reclassified threat.

goalsHistory.active: Clear the north-east lane, prepare fresh horses, and keep Arthas’s command traffic coherent as the column forms for Stratholme.
goalsHistory.underlying: Turn the prince’s will into working logistics quickly enough that the camp does not break under its own urgency.

goalsHistory.active: Keep the gate, officers, carts, and incoming command traffic from snarling as Arthas reaches Stratholme.
goalsHistory.underlying: Make the prince's arrival usable in practice by preventing sheer momentum from turning into immediate chaos.

emotionalStateHistory.state: Focused, efficient, and fully keyed to Arthas’s new priority rather than to his own opinion of the plague.
Tired, overused, and still exact. He reads as worn thin by tempo rather than shaken out of competence.
Hoarse, overrun, and still exact enough to point the right people to the right arch without wasting a second.

arcHistory.currentPosition: Chapter 11 introduces Falric as Arthas’s loyal command hinge: the officer who turns princely certainty into practical camp movement.
Chapter 15 keeps Falric as Arthas’s command hinge, but now under visibly heavier strain. He is no longer only the gatekeeper at the edge of action; he is one of the men making the next lane exist.
Chapter 16 keeps Falric in the same function but under higher visible wear. He is still Arthas's logistics hinge, now reduced almost entirely to movement management at the threshold of catastrophe.
